---
title: "Ch 6 ŌĆö Triangles (Similarity)"
subtitle: "Same shape, different size ŌĆö and the algebra that proves it"
lesson-id: "cbse10.triangles-similarity"
tier: "cbse10"
ncert-class: 10
ncert-chapter: 6
ncert-chapter-title: "Triangles"
board-exam: true
prerequisites: [cbse9.triangles, t1.ratio-proportion]
unlocks: [cbse10.trigonometry-intro, cbse10.coordinate-geometry, cbse11.straight-lines]
applied-domains: [game-dev, research]
estimated-minutes: 50
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you state when two triangles are *congruent*? See
   [`cbse9.triangles`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/07-triangles/index.qmd).
2. Can you simplify $\dfrac{12}{18}$ to $\dfrac{2}{3}$? See
   [`t1.ratio-proportion`](../../tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd).
3. Do you know the angle-sum property of a triangle ($180┬░$)?
4. Can you read off a ratio from a diagram (e.g. "AB : BC = 3 : 2")?

Three yeses and you are ready.
:::

## Touch

Take a printed photograph and a postage-stamp-sized version of the same
photograph. Lay them side by side. Pick **any** two corresponding points on
each ŌĆö say, two corners of the same building. Measure the distance between
those points on each photograph.

Repeat for another pair of corresponding points. Compute the ratios.

```{ojs}
viewof scale = Inputs.range([0.4, 2.5], {value: 1.6, step: 0.1, label: "Scale factor"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const w = 460, h = 280;
  const pts = [[40, 200], [120, 80], [180, 220]]; // small triangle
  const ptsBig = pts.map(([x, y]) => [w / 2 + (x - 110) * scale, h / 2 + (y - 150) * scale]);
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", w).attr("height", h)
    .style("background", "#fafafa").style("border", "1px solid #ddd");
  function drawTri(P, color) {
    const path = `M${P[0][0]},${P[0][1]} L${P[1][0]},${P[1][1]} L${P[2][0]},${P[2][1]} Z`;
    svg.append("path").attr("d", path).attr("fill", "none").attr("stroke", color)
      .attr("stroke-width", 2);
    P.forEach((pt, i) => {
      svg.append("circle").attr("cx", pt[0]).attr("cy", pt[1]).attr("r", 3).attr("fill", color);
    });
  }
  drawTri(pts, "#26a");
  drawTri(ptsBig, "#c33");
  // measure side AB on each
  const dist = (P, Q) => Math.hypot(P[0] - Q[0], P[1] - Q[1]);
  const ab1 = dist(pts[0], pts[1]);
  const ab2 = dist(ptsBig[0], ptsBig[1]);
  const bc1 = dist(pts[1], pts[2]);
  const bc2 = dist(ptsBig[1], ptsBig[2]);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", 10).attr("y", 14).attr("font-size", 12)
    .text(`AB ratio: ${(ab2 / ab1).toFixed(2)},   BC ratio: ${(bc2 / bc1).toFixed(2)}`);
  return svg.node();
}
```

The two ratios should be **the same number** ŌĆö the scale factor. Drag and
confirm.

## Wonder

1. The two triangles in the widget have the same *shape* but different
   *size*. What about the angles ŌĆö are they equal? Why?
2. If you saw a triangle from far away (small) and the same triangle close
   up (big), how could you tell if they are "the same" triangle?
3. Suppose you cut a triangle into a tiny one inside (with a line parallel
   to one side). Does the small triangle have the same angles as the big
   one?
4. If two triangles have the **same angles**, must their sides be in the
   same ratio?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) Yes, all three angles match. (2) Equal angles + ratio of sides
constant. (3) Yes ŌĆö that is the Basic Proportionality Theorem (BPT). (4)
Yes ŌĆö the AA criterion.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 ŌĆö A line parallel to a side
In $\triangle ABC$, a line through a point $D$ on $AB$ is drawn parallel to
$BC$, meeting $AC$ at $E$. If $AD = 2, DB = 3$, and $AE = 4$, find $EC$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
The Basic Proportionality Theorem says $\dfrac{AD}{DB} = \dfrac{AE}{EC}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{2}{3} = \dfrac{4}{EC} \Rightarrow EC = 6$.
</details>

### Problem 2 ŌĆö Why does BPT work?
Sketch $\triangle ABC$ with $DE \parallel BC$. Why are $\triangle ADE$ and
$\triangle ABC$ "the same shape"?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
List the angles of each. Use the fact that $DE \parallel BC$ and $AB$ is a
transversal.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\angle A$ is common. $\angle ADE = \angle ABC$ (corresponding angles, $DE
\parallel BC$ and $AB$ transversal). So all three angles match. The
triangles have **the same angles** ŌĆö they are the same shape, just at
different scale.
</details>

### Problem 3 ŌĆö AA criterion
$\triangle ABC$ has $\angle A = 50┬░, \angle B = 70┬░$. $\triangle DEF$ has
$\angle D = 50┬░, \angle E = 70┬░$. Without measuring, must $\triangle ABC$
and $\triangle DEF$ be the same shape?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
What is the third angle of each?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Both have $\angle C = \angle F = 60┬░$ (angle sum). All three angles match,
so the two triangles are the same shape ŌĆö **they are similar**. Just two
matching angles is enough; the third comes free.
</details>

### Problem 4 ŌĆö Ratio of sides
In two similar triangles, $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF$, with
$AB = 4, DE = 6$. If $BC = 5$, what is $EF$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
The scale factor is the same on every pair of corresponding sides.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Scale factor $= 6/4 = 3/2$. So $EF = 5 \cdot 3/2 = 7.5$.
</details>

### Problem 5 ŌĆö Ratio of areas
For the same triangles in Problem 4, what is the ratio of their **areas**?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
If sides scale by $k$, what does that do to the *area*?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 ŌĆö the technique</summary>
Area is a 2D quantity. Each side scales by $k$ ŌåÆ area scales by $k^2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{\text{area of } \triangle DEF}{\text{area of } \triangle ABC} =
\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{9}{4}$.
</details>

### Problem 6 ŌĆö Pythagoras as a similarity argument
A right triangle has legs $a, b$ and hypotenuse $c$. Drop a perpendicular
from the right-angle vertex to the hypotenuse. You get *three* right
triangles. Argue why each smaller triangle is **similar** to the big one.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
Each small triangle shares one acute angle with the big triangle. Plus the
right angle. AA criterion.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let the perpendicular hit the hypotenuse at $D$, splitting it into segments
$p$ (next to $a$) and $q$ (next to $b$). The two small triangles are similar
to the big one (each shares one acute angle and a right angle).
By similarity: $\dfrac{a}{c} = \dfrac{p}{a}$ and $\dfrac{b}{c} = \dfrac{q}
{b}$. So $a^2 = pc$ and $b^2 = qc$. Add:
$a^2 + b^2 = c(p + q) = c \cdot c = c^2$.
That is the **Pythagorean Theorem**, proved by similarity. (Euclid VI.31.)
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

Two triangles are **similar** (written $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF$)
if their corresponding angles are equal **and** their corresponding sides
are in the same ratio. Either condition implies the other.

**Criteria for similarity:**

- **AA (Angle-Angle):** Two pairs of equal angles Ō¤╣ similar (third comes free).
- **SAS (Side-Angle-Side):** Two pairs of sides proportional, included
  angles equal Ō¤╣ similar.
- **SSS (Side-Side-Side):** Three pairs of sides proportional Ō¤╣ similar.

**Basic Proportionality Theorem (BPT, also Thales' theorem).** A line
parallel to one side of a triangle, intersecting the other two sides in
distinct points, divides those sides in the same ratio.

**Areas of similar triangles.** If $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF$ with
ratio of sides $k$, then
$$\dfrac{\text{area}(\triangle ABC)}{\text{area}(\triangle DEF)} =
k^2.$$

:::

A verse:

> *Equal angles, sides in step ŌĆö that's similar in a flash.*
> *Two angles match? The third's the same ŌĆö it's free, no cash.*
> *Lengths grow by k, the area by k-squared makes its mark.*
> *Parallel lines on a triangle? Equal cuts, that's the trademark.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> In $\triangle ABC$, $D$ and $E$ are points on $AB$ and $AC$ respectively
> such that $DE \parallel BC$. If $AD = 6 \text{ cm}, DB = 9 \text{ cm}$,
> and $AE = 8 \text{ cm}$, find $AC$.

By BPT (since $DE \parallel BC$):
$\dfrac{AD}{DB} = \dfrac{AE}{EC}$, so $\dfrac{6}{9} = \dfrac{8}{EC}$, giving
$EC = 12 \text{ cm}$.

Therefore $AC = AE + EC = 8 + 12 = \mathbf{20 \text{ cm}}$.

### Practice ŌĆö Warm

**W1.** State the AA similarity criterion.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
If two angles of one triangle equal two angles of another, the triangles
are similar.
</details>

**W2.** $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF$ with $AB = 5, DE = 7$. Ratio of
their areas?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(5/7)^2 = 25/49$.
</details>

**W3.** In $\triangle ABC$, $DE \parallel BC$ with $D$ on $AB, E$ on $AC$,
$AD = 4, DB = 6, EC = 9$. Find $AE$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{AD}{DB} = \dfrac{AE}{EC} \Rightarrow \dfrac{4}{6} = \dfrac{AE}{9}
\Rightarrow AE = 6$.
</details>

### Practice ŌĆö Working

**P1.** Two poles of heights $6 \text{ m}$ and $11 \text{ m}$ stand on
level ground. The distance between their feet is $12 \text{ m}$. Find the
distance between their tops.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
Right triangle: horizontal leg $12$, vertical leg $11 - 6 = 5$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
By Pythagoras: $\sqrt{12^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{169} = 13 \text{ m}$.
</details>

**P2.** A vertical pole of length $6 \text{ m}$ casts a shadow of $4 \text{
m}$ on the ground. At the same time, a tower casts a shadow of $28 \text{
m}$. Find the height of the tower.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
Pole/shadow and tower/shadow form similar right triangles (sun's rays are
parallel).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{6}{4} = \dfrac{H}{28} \Rightarrow H = 42 \text{ m}$.
</details>

**P3.** In $\triangle ABC$, $\angle B = 90┬░$. $D$ is on $AC$ such that $BD
\perp AC$. If $AD = 4, DC = 9$, find $BD$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
The geometric-mean / altitude-on-hypotenuse fact: $BD^2 = AD \cdot DC$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$BD^2 = 4 \cdot 9 = 36 \Rightarrow BD = 6$.
</details>

### Practice ŌĆö Stretch

**S1.** In a trapezium $ABCD$ with $AB \parallel DC$, the diagonals $AC$
and $BD$ intersect at $O$. Prove that $\dfrac{OA}{OC} = \dfrac{OB}{OD}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
Look for similar triangles formed by the diagonals.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\triangle AOB \sim \triangle COD$ by AA: $\angle AOB = \angle COD$
(vertically opposite); $\angle OAB = \angle OCD$ (alternate angles, $AB
\parallel DC$). By similarity, $\dfrac{OA}{OC} = \dfrac{OB}{OD}$
(corresponding sides).
</details>

**S2.** In $\triangle ABC$, $D$ is the midpoint of $BC$. From $D$ a line is
drawn parallel to $AB$, meeting $AC$ at $E$. Show that $E$ is the midpoint
of $AC$, and that $DE = \frac{1}{2}AB$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
Apply BPT in $\triangle ABC$ with the line $DE$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
By BPT, $\dfrac{CD}{DB} = \dfrac{CE}{EA}$. But $CD = DB$ (D is midpoint), so
$CE = EA$ ŌĆö $E$ is the midpoint of $AC$. For the length: $\triangle CDE
\sim \triangle CBA$ with ratio $1/2$, so $DE = \frac{1}{2}AB$. (This is the
**Midpoint Theorem**.)
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**Q1 (1 mark ŌĆö MCQ).** $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle PQR$ with $AB/PQ =
3/4$. Then area ($\triangle ABC$) / area ($\triangle PQR$) is:
(a) $3/4$  (b) $9/16$  (c) $4/3$  (d) $16/9$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(3/4)^2 = 9/16$. **(b).**
</details>

**Q2 (1 mark ŌĆö assertion-reason).** Assertion (A): If two triangles are
similar, their perimeters are in the same ratio as their corresponding
sides. Reason (R): The ratio of areas of two similar triangles is the
square of the ratio of corresponding sides.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
A is true (perimeters scale linearly). R is true. R does **not** explain A
ŌĆö area is a different fact. Correct mark: A and R both true, but R is not
the reason for A.
</details>

**Q3 (2 marks).** In $\triangle ABC$, $DE \parallel BC$. If $AD = x, DB = x
- 2, AE = x + 2, EC = x - 1$, find $x$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
BPT: $\dfrac{x}{x - 2} = \dfrac{x + 2}{x - 1}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Cross-multiply: $x(x - 1) = (x + 2)(x - 2)$, i.e. $x^2 - x = x^2 - 4
\Rightarrow x = 4$.
</details>

**Q4 (2 marks).** State and prove that the ratio of the areas of two
similar triangles is equal to the square of the ratio of their
corresponding sides. (For two specific triangles you may use a sketch.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
Standard NCERT theorem. Use the formula $\frac{1}{2}$ base ├Ś height on each.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle PQR$ with corresponding sides in ratio
$k$. Drop altitudes $AM$ (from $A$ on $BC$) and $PN$ (from $P$ on $QR$).
Then $\triangle ABM \sim \triangle PQN$ (AA ŌĆö both have right angle at $M,
N$ and $\angle B = \angle Q$), so $AM / PN = AB/PQ = k$. Also
$BC/QR = k$. Therefore
$$\dfrac{\text{ar}(\triangle ABC)}{\text{ar}(\triangle PQR)} =
\dfrac{\frac{1}{2}BC \cdot AM}{\frac{1}{2}QR \cdot PN} = k \cdot k = k^2.
\square$$
</details>

**Q5 (3 marks).** In a $\triangle ABC$, right-angled at $B$, $BD \perp AC$
with $AB = 12, BC = 5$. Find $BD$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
$AC = ?$ first, by Pythagoras. Then use area = $\frac{1}{2}AB \cdot BC =
\frac{1}{2}AC \cdot BD$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$AC = \sqrt{144 + 25} = 13$. Area = $\frac{1}{2} \cdot 12 \cdot 5 = 30$.
Also area $= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 13 \cdot BD$, so $BD = \dfrac{60}{13}$.
</details>

**Q6 (3 marks).** In $\triangle ABC$, $D$ and $E$ are on $AB$ and $AC$
respectively, such that $DE \parallel BC$ and $\dfrac{AD}{DB} = \dfrac{2}
{3}$. If $\text{ar}(\triangle ABC) = 50 \text{ cm}^2$, find ar($\triangle
ADE$).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
$\triangle ADE \sim \triangle ABC$. Find the ratio of sides first.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{AD}{AB} = \dfrac{2}{2 + 3} = \dfrac{2}{5}$. Area ratio = $(2/5)^2 =
4/25$. So ar($\triangle ADE$) = $\dfrac{4}{25} \cdot 50 = 8 \text{ cm}^2$.
</details>

**Q7 (5 marks ŌĆö long answer).** State and prove the **Basic Proportionality
Theorem** (BPT). Use it to prove that if a line divides any two sides of a
triangle in the same ratio, then the line must be parallel to the third
side (the converse).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ŌĆö a nudge</summary>
Forward: drop perpendiculars from $D, E$ to $BC$ for the area-ratio proof.
Converse: assume the ratio-equality; show the line *is* parallel by
contradiction (or by uniqueness).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Forward (BPT).** In $\triangle ABC$, let $DE \parallel BC$ with $D$ on
$AB$, $E$ on $AC$. Drop perpendiculars from $D$ to $BC$ at $M$ and from $E$
to $BC$ at $N$. Then ar($\triangle ADE$) shares an altitude with $\triangle
DBE$ (from $E$, common vertex). So
$$\dfrac{\text{ar}(\triangle ADE)}{\text{ar}(\triangle DBE)} = \dfrac{AD}
{DB}.$$
Similarly with vertex $D$:
$$\dfrac{\text{ar}(\triangle ADE)}{\text{ar}(\triangle DEC)} = \dfrac{AE}
{EC}.$$
But $\triangle DBE$ and $\triangle DEC$ have the **same** base $DE$ and
**equal heights** (because $DE \parallel BC$), so they have equal area.
Hence $\dfrac{AD}{DB} = \dfrac{AE}{EC}$. $\square$

**Converse.** Suppose a line through $D$ on $AB$ meets $AC$ at $E$ with
$\dfrac{AD}{DB} = \dfrac{AE}{EC}$. Draw $DE'$ parallel to $BC$ from $D$,
meeting $AC$ at $E'$. By BPT, $\dfrac{AD}{DB} = \dfrac{AE'}{E'C}$. So
$\dfrac{AE}{EC} = \dfrac{AE'}{E'C}$, which forces $E = E'$. Hence $DE
\parallel BC$. $\square$
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar 6.3 Q1ŌĆōQ12 are direct
similarity-criterion applications like Q3; Exemplar 6.4 Q1ŌĆōQ12 are full
proofs like Q7.

**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE 2020 Set 1, Q31 is essentially Q7 (BPT
proof). CBSE 2023 Standard, Q35 mirrors Q6.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Similar means same size."** No ŌĆö similar means same *shape*. Same
   size with same shape is *congruent*. Congruent Ō¤╣ similar; similar Ō¤╣
   congruent only if the scale factor is $1$.
2. **"BPT needs the line to bisect."** No ŌĆö BPT works for *any* line
   parallel to a side. It divides the other two sides in the *same* ratio
   (whatever that ratio is).
3. **"Area ratio equals side ratio."** No ŌĆö area ratio equals *square* of
   side ratio. A common board mistake worth Ōé╣2-3.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state and prove BPT cleanly, without notes?
2. Did Q5 and Q6 fall out cleanly with at most one hint?
3. Could you spot a similarity argument inside an unfamiliar diagram (the
   Pythagoras-by-similarity in *Discover, Problem 6*)?

- **3 yes:** move on.
- **2 yes:** redo Q4 and Q7. They are full proofs; the board paper is
  unforgiving on these.
- **0ŌĆō1 yes:** the area-ratio proof in *Discover, Problem 5* and
  *Q4/Q7* is the load-bearing skill. Re-walk it.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`cbse9.triangles`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/07-triangles/index.qmd) ŌĆö
  congruence criteria.
- [`t1.ratio-proportion`](../../tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd) ŌĆö
  ratios as numerical objects.

### What this unlocks

- [`cbse10.trigonometry-intro`](../08a-trigonometry-intro/index.qmd) ŌĆö
  trigonometric ratios are exactly the side-ratios of similar right
  triangles.
- [`cbse10.coordinate-geometry`](../07-coordinate-geometry/index.qmd) ŌĆö
  the section formula uses similar-triangle reasoning.
- [`cbse11.straight-lines`](../../tier-cbse-11-class-11/09-straight-lines/index.qmd).

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 10, Chapter 6 ŌĆö Triangles** (NCERT 2023 rationalised
  syllabus). Covers Sections 6.1ŌĆō6.4: similarity criteria, BPT and
  converse, areas of similar triangles. (Sections 6.5/6.6 on Pythagoras
  and its converse were rationalised out, but the *similarity-based*
  proof in Discover Problem 6 is now the canonical route.)
- **Sister synthesis lessons:**
  - [`t2.similarity-scale`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/13-similarity-scale/index.qmd) ŌĆö
    similarity from scratch.
  - [`t2.pythagorean-theorem`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/10-pythagorean-theorem/index.qmd) ŌĆö
    the Pythagorean theorem in isolation.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Every 3D engine renders distant objects as
*similar* (smaller) copies of nearer objects ŌĆö the projection
mathematics is exactly the similarity scaling you've practised.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Self-similarity (fractals ŌĆö Mandelbrot, Sierpi┼äski) is the
deeper version of "same shape at every scale". The similarity tools you
built in this chapter are the entry to fractal geometry.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Kiselev (proof exposition for BPT and the area
  theorem); Russian school (problem-first); Bh─üskara (compact *Name*).
- **Source notes:** the BPT proof in Q7 is the standard NCERT proof;
  Discover Problem 6 follows Euclid VI.31.
:::
